Name: information_theoretic_neural_compression
Title: Exploring the Information-Theoretic Limits of Neural Network Compression
Short Hypothesis: Neural networks can be compressed to a fraction of their original size while preserving task-relevant information, and this compression can be quantified and bounded using rate-distortion theory and statistical learning bounds.
Related Work: Previous work has focused on empirical methods for neural network compression, such as quantization and pruning. However, these approaches lack a clear understanding of the fundamental limits of compression. Our proposal seeks to address this gap by applying information-theoretic principles to derive achievable rates and distortion bounds for neural network compression.
Abstract: This proposal explores the information-theoretic limits of neural network compression. We aim to derive achievable rates and distortion bounds for compressing neural networks while preserving task-relevant information. Our approach combines rate-distortion theory and statistical learning bounds to quantify the fundamental limits of compression. We will investigate the trade-off between compression rate and distortion, and evaluate the efficiency of different compression methods relative to these limits. Our results will provide a theoretical foundation for understanding the limits of neural network compression and guiding the development of more efficient compression methods.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments to evaluate the compression efficiency of different methods, including quantization and pruning, using metrics such as bits-per-weight and distortion-rate curves. We will also investigate the effect of different distortion measures and source distributions on the compression limits. Our experiments will be designed to test the achievability of our derived bounds and to identify the most efficient compression methods.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The main risk of this proposal is that the theoretical bounds we derive may not be tight, or that the compression methods we evaluate may not achieve these bounds in practice. Additionally, our results may be sensitive to the choice of distortion measure and source distribution. To mitigate these risks, we will carefully evaluate the assumptions underlying our bounds and ensure that our experiments are designed to test these assumptions.
